title: Defining Unique, Redundant, and Synergistic Quantum Information
authors: Sean Ericson, Hailin Wang, S. J. van Enk
abstract: We extend the classical ideas of the Partial Information Decomposition (PID) to the quantum domain and quantify unique, redundant, and synergistic quantum information. We show that unique information plays the central role in quantum error correction codes: any erasure-correctable subset of encoding qubits must contain zero unique information. Synergistic information between two disjoint subsets of encoding qubits appears when a logical operation is supported on the whole set but not on the subsets separately. In a different application of our PID, we show that redundant quantum information is the crucial feature of the decoherence mechanism proposed by Zurek \textit{et al.} to explain how the classical world emerges out of the quantum world.
summary: This work generalizes the concept of decomposing total information into unique, redundant, and synergistic parts for quantum systems. By applying this Quantum PID framework to error correction codes and decoherence models, it reveals fundamental roles: unique information governs error correction, while redundancy is key to understanding how quantum mechanics yields classical reality.
main_problem: The paper extends classical Partial Information Decomposition (PID) concepts—unique, redundant, and synergistic information—to quantify these quantities in the quantum domain.
main_result: It establishes that unique information is central to quantum error correction codes, while redundant information plays a crucial role in describing the decoherence mechanism.
method: The analysis uses superdense coding capacity ($C_{AB}$) as the primary tool to define and calculate the quantum PID components for various multipartite states.
model_or_system: The study applies these concepts to Quantum Error Correction (QEC) codes (e.g., bit-flip, five-qubit code) and the decoherence process modeled by environmental interactions.
key_observables: Unique quantum information ($C_{	ext{unq}}$), redundant capacity ($C_{	ext{red}}$), synergistic capacity ($C_{	ext{syn}}$), and superdense coding capacities ($C_{AB}, C_A, C_B$).
important_parameters: The entanglement structure of the state ($\lambda$), the number of supported logical operators ($l_A$), and the interaction parameters in decoherence models.
main_assumptions: Multiple sensible definitions for quantum PID exist; specific results rely on analyzing pure states or assuming certain code structures (e.g., stabilizer codes).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate superdense capacities and C-PID components for decoherence models, showing plateaus and dependence on interaction strength; tables summarize capacity combinations for various QEC codes.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the quantum PID using general bipartite states to applying it specifically to QEC codes (analyzing subsets A and B) and finally applying it to the decoherence model to explain classical emergence.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous mathematical framework for quantifying how information is encoded, lost, or shared in complex physical processes like decoherence, which is central to understanding quantum-to-classical transitions relevant to AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Collective and individual two-bit capacities for the states ρMB(|0⟩, |0⟩; p) and ρMB(|+⟩, |0⟩; p). For p = 0, 1, the target is maximally entangled with one of the parties, hence the joint capacity is maximized and equal to that party’s in- dividual capacity. The individual capacities are independent of |ψ⟩and |ϕ⟩, although different choices for these “spectator” states do slightly affect the joint capacity.

FIG. 2. C-PID for two instances of the mixed-Bell state. In a) the spectator states are |ψ⟩= |ϕ⟩= |0⟩. The symmetry of this state precludes the possibility of Alice and Bob simultaneously having unique capacity. In b) the spectator states are |ψ⟩= |+⟩ and |ϕ⟩= |0⟩. The asymmetry in this case allows for a range of p values (0.47 ≲p ≲0.52) over which both Alice and Bob have nonzero unique capacity. Note that in this case the state is not invariant under A ↔B and p ↔1 −p, hence the slight asymmetry in the plot.

FIG. 3. Distributions of Cunq, Cred, and Csyn for 10000 random [[5, 1]] encodings for the state given in (33) with λ = 1/3. The corresponding values of the four C-PID quantities for the same partitions of the exact five-qubit code are displayed as vertical lines.

FIG. 4. Mutual information between system and environ- ment fragment as a function of fragment size with λ = 1/3, sin α = 0.85, and N = 100. For moderately sized fragments (m around 15) the fragment/system mutual information sat- urates at the system entropy. The fragment size needed to reach the plateau is determined by the imperfection parame- ter α.

FIG. 5. a) Collective and individual superdense capacities for the decoherence model as a function of the number of environment qubits in Alice’s possession, with λ = 1/3, sin α = 0.85, and N = 100 environment qubits total. The collective two-bit capacity depends only on the state of the target and not how the environment qubits are partitioned between Alice and Bob. It is maximized for λ = 1/2. As Alice and Bob interact with the target in the same way, their individual two-bit capacities are symmetric under A ↔B and m ↔N −m. b) C-PID for the original decoherence model two-bit capacity.
